query: is pf-05190457 an inverse agonist of the ghrelin receptor

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Because ghrelin is involved in reward-processing, the ghrelin system is also being studied as a target in the context of alcohol misuse disorder. Rodent studies have found that administration of ghrelin increased alcohol intake, while ghrelin receptor blockade had the opposite effect. Preliminary studies involving heavy users of alcohol have found that ghrelin administration increases alcohol cravings, while PF-5190457, a GHS-R1-alpha-blocker, appeared to reduce cravings. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Other Issues -- New Drugs/Therapies Being Developed to Tackle Obesity
retrieved_docs.content: These are not yet FDA-approved drugs but target parts of the hedonistic and homeostatic pathways in the peripheral and central nervous system to induce weight loss. They include tesofensine-a serotonin-noradrenaline-dopamine reuptake inhibitor, RNA inhibitor against RIP140, CDDO-imidazolide, antibodies against ghrelin, high dose systemic PYY antagonists, cetilistat-a gastric and pancreatic lipase inhibitor, beloranib-MetAP2 inhibitor, KD026-microsomal transfer protein inhibitor in the intestine, fecal microbiota transplant, bile acid receptors-TGR5, FXR, GSK1521498-selective mu-opioid receptor antagonist, PRX-07034-a 5-HT6 antagonist, SNAP-94847-MCH-1 antagonist, oxyntomodulin-a glucagon + GLP 1 agonist, ghrelin antagonist and vaccines, GIP receptor antagonist, monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL), fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH)-appetite suppression-degradation of endocannabinoids anandamide, 2-arachidonic acid glycerol, a long-acting amylin analog, an FGF21 analog, licogliflozin-SGLT1 inhibitor, PYY 3-36 analog, oxytocin receptor agonist and a Type 4 FGF receptor antagonist. [9] [8] [11] [13] [31] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Inhibitory Peptide -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: GIP receptors have also become therapeutic targets in the development of new antidiabetic medications. Tirzepatide, a dual GLP-1 and GIP receptor agonist, has demonstrated superior efficacy in weight reduction and glycemic control compared with GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide, particularly at higher doses of 10 and 15 mg. [47] [48] Tirzepatide has also received U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval for the treatment of obstructive sleep apnea, and ongoing clinical studies are investigating its potential benefits in several other conditions, including polycystic ovarian syndrome, psoriasis, cardiovascular disease, and alcohol use disorder. Furthermore, triple-receptor agonists such as reatrutide are in phase III clinical trials and may provide additional therapeutic benefit in weight management and T2DM.

retrieved_docs.title: Setmelanotide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Setmelanotide is a type of MC4R agonist with 20-fold less activity at the melanocortin-3 receptor (MC3R) and melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R). The MC4R can be endogenously activated by the binding of melanocyte-stimulating hormones (MSH), which are involved in responding to acute signal regulations of hunger and satiety. [8] [9] Setmelanotide acts on the MC4R pathway to reverse hyperphagia and promote weight loss through decreased caloric intake and increased energy expenditure. [2] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Issues of Concern -- Retatrutide
retrieved_docs.content: Retatrutide (manufactured by Eli Lilly) represents the next evolution in incretin-based therapy as a triple GLP-1/GIP/glucagon receptor agonist currently in phase 3 trials. Phase 2 data demonstrated an unprecedented 24.2% weight loss at 48 weeks with the highest dose, surpassing the weight loss achieved with all currently available medications. The triple mechanism targets complementary pathways in glucose homeostasis, appetite regulation, and energy expenditure, providing synergistic effects that translate into superior clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Mu Receptors -- Clinical Significance -- Research Perspectives
retrieved_docs.content: Further research is needed to identify the precise residues responsible for ligand selectivity, the mechanism of ligand-dependent endocytosis (eg, the phosphorylation patterns), and the potential modulation of the G protein/cAMP pathway against down-regulation mechanisms. Other research involves allosteric modulators of MOR activity, including compounds BMS-986123 and BMS-986124, which are silent allosteric modulators (SAMs). These agents neither potentiate nor inhibit the actions of an orthosteric agonist, although they can block the effects of the specific positive allosteric modulators (PAMs).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Obesity Neurohormonal Appetite And Satiety Control -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Ghrelin and leptin exist as key hormones with regulatory effects of clinical significance in treating various disorders. In cancer cachexia, ghrelin has already shown promise as a therapeutic option with its anti-inflammatory action on cancer cells. Through its effects on muscle catabolism, anti-apoptotic mechanism, and reducing the adverse effects of chemotherapy, ghrelin may help treat cancer cachexia. [35] The effects of ghrelin on the rest of the body's metabolism are vast, and avenues for anti-inflammation, improvement of cardiac performance, and stress modulation are continually under investigation. [36] Synthetic ghrelin-receptor agonist analogs like Anamorelin have shown beneficial effects. [37] The effects and circulation of leptin have also been experimented with regarding weight loss. [38] Many of these studies are still undergoing clinical trials, hoping they will have significant clinical benefits in the future.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Issues of Concern -- Survodutide
retrieved_docs.content: Survodutide (manufactured by Boehringer Ingelheim) is a novel dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist that achieved 18.7% weight loss in phase 2 trials. Adding glucagon receptor agonism to GLP-1 activity enhances metabolic rate and energy expenditure, providing a complementary mechanism to appetite suppression, thereby achieving superior weight loss outcomes. Survodutide has demonstrated direct hepatic effects, including enhanced fat mobilization, lipolysis, increased energy expenditure, and a reduction in hepatic steatosis and fibrotic processes, in clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Consequences of Weight Reduction -- Function -- Pharmacological and Surgical Intervention For Weight Loss and Metabolic Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Recent advancements in technologies and metabolomics have helped our understanding of the mechanistic pathways and metabolites that are mediators of an increase in BMI and weight gain. However, only five drug therapies have been approved for obesity treatment thus far. [34] These therapies are based on the use of analogs that manipulate gut hormones, facilitating weight loss. Presently, glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) analogs are used as monotherapy, unimolecular agonists for gastric inhibitory peptide receptor (GIP), GLP-1 receptor, or glucagon receptor are used to induce weight loss. Furthermore, leptin analogs, ghrelin antagonists, amylin mimetics and melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R), and neuropeptide Y (NPY) antagonists that suppress appetite have demonstrated success in preclinical and clinical trials. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- 1. Tirzepatide
retrieved_docs.content: Controversies surrounding the drug and trials: Some studies have shown that GIP is, in fact, obesogenic and there have been GIP antagonists developed to tackle obesity. GIP itself can contribute to hyperglycemia because of the stimulation of glucagon and can inhibit the effect of GLP-1 on decreasing food intake. Hence at least per laboratory data, using a GIP agonist is of limited utility. However, the trial investigators argue that GIP contributes more to the incretin effect than GLP-1, and GIP-induced weight loss is via glucagon's anti-lipogenic and anorexic action. Thus having a dual agonist was more effective than a single agent. Another criticism is that the dose of GLP-1 agonists used in the SURPASS trials to show superiority is suboptimal compared to the dose currently being studied to treat obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Once secreted, ghrelin exerts its orexigenic effects by binding to the ghrelin/growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1-alpha). [2] GHS-R1-alpha receptors appear on the arcuate and the ventromedial nuclei of the hypothalamus; these cells also host the receptors for leptin, a satiety hormone that opposes the effects of ghrelin. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Tirzepatide is a synthetic polypeptide dual agonist for GLP-1 and GIP. Tirzepatide, "twincretin," exhibits distinct characteristics from GLP-1 receptor agonists. [8] The medication comprises 39 amino acids and is an analog of the gastric inhibitory polypeptide. Functionally, tirzepatide stimulates insulin release from the pancreas and reduces hyperglycemia. In addition, tirzepatide also increases the levels of adiponectin.

retrieved_docs.title: Setmelanotide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Setmelanotide can bind to and activates multiple melanocortin receptors, including melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R), melanocortin-3 receptor (MC3R), and melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R) selectively over melanocortin-2 receptor (MC2R) and melanocortin-5 receptor (MC5R). [6] These melanocortin receptor systems consist of five 7-transmembrane G-protein coupled receptors, which are involved in many key physiological functions of the body, including feeding behavior, energy balance, sexual function and behavior, cardiovascular function, kidney function, immune response, etc. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Function -- Setmelanotide
retrieved_docs.content: Setmelanotide (IMCIVREE)  represents precision medicine in obesity treatment, specifically targeting rare genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin-4 receptor agonism (see Table 7 ). This highly specialized medication addresses the underlying pathophysiology of monogenic obesity disorders by restoring hypothalamic appetite regulation pathways disrupted by genetic mutations in the proopiomelanocortin pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist, indicating that it functions by blocking hormone receptors. Atosiban is identified as a substance with a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action and is also categorized as a hormone antagonist. These relationships show that both PD 134308 and atosiban share the functional property of antagonizing hormone receptors, a characteristic that is important for therapeutic approaches aimed at modulating hormone‑mediated physiological processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist (isa), placing it within the broader category of antagonisté hormonů. Atosiban is also identified as a member of this hormone‑antagonist class (inverse_isa), meaning it functions as a specific agent that blocks hormonal activity. Both PD 134308 and Atosiban therefore share the common pharmacological property of antagonizing hormone receptors, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2457
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist. Atosiban is likewise classified as a hormone antagonist and is a synthetic analogue that is derived from the natural peptide vasotocin. Both PD 134308 and atosiban belong to the hormone‑antagonist class, indicating that they act by blocking specific hormonal pathways, and the link between atosiban and vasotocin highlights the latter’s origin as a modified form of the endogenous hormone. This shared classification informs their pharmacological use as agents that inhibit hormone‑mediated effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1318
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products are a class of pharmacologic agents that target central nervous system pathways to reduce appetite and promote weight loss, and this class includes both setmelanotide and sibutramine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1312
retrieved_docs.content: Atosiban is classified as a substance that exerts its effects through a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, placing it within the broader category of hormone antagonists. This classification highlights its role in blocking specific peptide hormone receptors, which is central to its therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Lixisenatide is classified as a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue, belonging to the therapeutic class of GLP‑1 analogues that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1 to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. As a member of this drug class, lixisenatide is employed in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus, often in combination with other antidiabetic agents, and its pharmacologic actions are consistent with those of other GLP‑1 analogues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise several agents, including exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. These GLP‑1 analogues are often combined with basal insulins to enhance glycaemic control in patients with diabetes. The fixed‑ratio combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide incorporates liraglutide, while the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains lixisenatide. By pairing GLP‑1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide or lixisenatide with long‑acting insulins, clinicians can exploit complementary mechanisms—insulin’s basal glucose‑lowering effect and GLP‑1 analogues’ glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delayed gastric emptying, and appetite suppression—to achieve more effective and convenient diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: The substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, known as atosiban, functions as an inverse ISA, indicating that it antagonizes peptide hormone receptors while exhibiting inverse agonist properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Atosiban is classified as a substance that exerts a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, meaning it blocks the activity of peptide hormone receptors. This classification is expressed through an inverse‑isa relationship that links atosiban to the broader category of peptide‑hormone‑receptor antagonists. Additionally, atosiban is mapped from vasotocin, indicating that its chemical structure or pharmacologic profile is derived from the peptide hormone vasotocin. Together, these relationships define atosiban as a vasotocin‑derived agent designed to inhibit peptide hormone signaling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1165
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives is a biochemical category that encompasses several specific compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine. These three substances share the common characteristic of being either amino acids themselves or closely related metabolic derivatives. In contrast, metreleptin—a recombinant leptin analog used in the treatment of leptin deficiency—is not a member of the amino acids and derivatives class, reflecting its distinct protein‑based structure and therapeutic role. Additionally, glutamine is correctly classified as an amino acid rather than being derived from or having a parent relationship with proglumide; proglumide is an unrelated cholecystokinin‑antagonist drug. Recognizing the proper classification of these molecules supports accurate biochemical understanding and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 816
retrieved_docs.content: The category “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES” includes agents classified as estrogen antagonists (antagonisté estrogenu). Estrogen antagonists are a sub‑category of the broader class of hormone antagonists (antagonisté hormonů). Within this hierarchy, the synthetic compound centchroman is identified as an estrogen antagonist, while the oxytocin‑receptor antagonist atosiban is recognized as a member of the hormone‑antagonist class. The investigational agent PD 134308 is also classified as a hormone antagonist. Additionally, the grouping “hormone antagonists and related agents” is placed under the umbrella of estrogen antagonists, further illustrating the nested relationships among these pharmacologic categories. This structured classification helps clarify how various hormonal‑targeting drugs are related and supports informed therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist (antagonisté hormonů). Atosiban is also a member of the hormone antagonist class, as indicated by the inverse “isa” relationship. Estrogen antagonists (antagonisté estrogenu) are a subcategory of hormone antagonists, establishing a hierarchical link between estrogen‑specific antagonism and broader hormone antagonism. Centchroman is identified as an estrogen antagonist, placing it within both the estrogen antagonist subcategory and the larger grouping of “hormone antagonists and related agents.” This structure shows that PD 134308 and atosiban act as general hormone antagonists, while centchroman functions as a more specific estrogen antagonist, all of which are organized under the overarching category of hormone antagonists and related agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Hormone antagonists (antagonisté hormonů) represent a pharmacologic class that includes specific agents such as mifepristone and atosiban. Mifepristone is an example of a hormone antagonist that blocks progesterone receptors and is used in medical termination of pregnancy and other therapeutic contexts. Atosiban, described through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, is also a hormone antagonist; it acts as an oxytocin receptor blocker to inhibit uterine contractions and is employed in the management of preterm labor. Recognizing both mifepristone and atosiban as members of the hormone antagonist class highlights their shared mechanism of opposing endogenous hormonal signaling while underscoring their distinct clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products constitute a pharmacologic class that promotes weight loss by influencing central nervous system pathways involved in appetite and energy balance. This class includes the combination therapy bupropion / naltrexone, the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide, and the serotonin‑norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor sibutramine. Bupropion / naltrexone works by modulating reward and satiety circuits, setmelanotide targets genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin signaling, and sibutramine reduces appetite by increasing synaptic concentrations of serotonin and norepinephrine. Together, these centrally acting agents illustrate the diverse mechanisms by which central pathways can be leveraged to treat obesity, highlighting the importance of individualized therapeutic selection based on efficacy, safety profile, and underlying patient characteristics.
